findings.issue: Non-atomic migration + sentinel pair creates a lost-update / data-destruction window across process death. `migrateIfNeeded()` performs TWO separate DataStore.updateData calls: (1) the big rekey block that rewrites per_model_overrides from name to modelId, and (2) `appSettings.markSettingsMigrated()` which sets settings_keys_migrated=true via a second updateData. The KDoc claims `DataStore's atomicity guarantees that a crash mid-migration leaves either the old map or the new map`, but that guarantee covers only the first call — not the pair. Failure mode: if the process is killed (OOM, user force-stop, SIGKILL) between call (1) and call (2), on next boot the sentinel is still false, migrateIfNeeded() runs again, re-reads the already-rekeyed overrides, builds a nameToModelId lookup whose keys are Phase-1 names, sees every existing key as orphan (because the stored keys are now HF modelIds that don't match the `name` lookup keys), logs each one as orphan via ErrorLog, and drops them via `clearPerModelOverrides().putAllPerModelOverrides(emptyMap)` — destroying all user per-model settings with no recovery path. This is not a theoretical edge case: Android routinely kills backgrounded app processes, and DataStore writes happen asynchronously; the window between the two updateData calls is wall-clock observable. This is an integrity failure (OWASP A08) rather than a classical security vuln — there is no attacker — but the effect is the same as a malicious data wipe.
findings.fix:
Fold the sentinel write into the same updateData lambda that performs the rekey, so DataStore's single-writer atomicity covers both:

```kotlin
dataStore.updateData { current ->
  val old = current.perModelOverridesMap
  val remapped = LinkedHashMap<String, PerModelSettings>(old.size)
  for ((oldKey, value) in old) {
    val newKey = nameToModelId[oldKey]
    if (newKey.isNullOrEmpty()) {
      errorLog.e(ERROR_COMPONENT, "orphan per-model-settings key dropped during Phase 3 rekey: $oldKey")
      continue
    }
    remapped[newKey] = value
  }
  current.toBuilder()
    .clearPerModelOverrides()
    .putAllPerModelOverrides(remapped)
    .setSettingsKeysMigrated(true)   // <-- sentinel flipped atomically
    .build()
}
// drop the trailing appSettings.markSettingsMigrated() call
```

After this change, also drop the now-unnecessary tests relying on a separate markSettingsMigrated() call and verify that `sentinel_preventsRerun` still holds. Alternatively, if the two-call shape must stay for layering reasons, make the rekey idempotent by also treating an empty `nameToModelId` intersection as a no-op: `if (old.isEmpty() || old.keys.none { it in nameToModelId }) return@updateData current` — but the atomic single-write is simpler and strictly stronger. CWE-362 (Race Condition) / CWE-662 (Improper Synchronization).

findings.issue: `AllowedModel.name` is the only field that flows into DataStore keys during Phase 1/2 operation and into the orphan-log description during Phase 3 migration, yet it is the ONE field that AllowlistLoader does NOT validate. `modelId`, `modelFile`, `commitHash`, `sizeInBytes` all have regex/range guards (MODEL_ID_REGEX, MODEL_FILE_REGEX, COMMIT_HASH_REGEX, 1..10GB range). `name` passes through Gson reflection unchecked — free-form string, may contain newlines, control chars, NUL bytes, arbitrary length. Impact is currently low because (a) allowlist JSON is bundled in the signed APK, so tampering requires repackaging — same trust boundary as the code; (b) ErrorLog.sanitize() already neutralizes \n\r\t and truncates to 500 chars before writing the orphan line, so errors.log cannot be corrupted by a weird name. DataStore stores `name` verbatim as a proto map key (Phase 1/2), but proto3 string map keys are length-prefixed and tolerate any UTF-8 bytes — no injection surface. So no exploitable path exists today. However, defense-in-depth: Phase 3 makes `name` even less load-bearing (modelId takes over as the canonical key), so this is a good moment to add a `name` regex guard in AllowlistLoader so future consumers don't inherit an unchecked string.

Orphan keys are logged one-line-per-key. If the allowlist ever shrinks substantially between versions (unlikely: litert-community pin is stable) AND a user has many overrides, the migration could emit N orphan lines to errors.log. errors.log has a 2 MB rotation guard in ErrorLog, so the blast radius is bounded — but per-key logging is slightly chatty. No action needed; just flagging that the current design is correct (per-key visibility for debug support) and the rotation cap keeps it safe.
findings.fix: No change. Acceptable trade-off for local-only debug logging. If desired later, switch to a single aggregated line: `errorLog.e("settings-io", "Phase 3 rekey dropped ${orphans.size} orphan keys: ${orphans.take(10).joinToString()}${if (orphans.size > 10) \"...\" else \"\"}")`.

findings.issue: Phase 3 wires real `activeModelId()` and `downloadedModels()` (stable HF repo ids like `litert-community/Gemma-...`) into the exportable log .txt that users share via the OS share sheet. Phase 2.5 stubbed these to null/empty. This is NOT a privacy regression: (a) the user explicitly initiates the export via the Share action; (b) HF repo ids are public, deterministic identifiers of the bundled allowlist and are not user-derived PII — they expose only which curated models the user has downloaded, information which is already inferable from app storage size and allowlist version; (c) `modelId` is strictly whitelisted to `litert-community/[A-Za-z0-9._-]+`, so no free-form user text can leak here; (d) there are no user-supplied model names being exported — `AndroidDeviceInfoProvider` explicitly uses `model.modelId`, not `model.name`. The crash-process path keeps the empty stub, so no model data leaks via the crash pipeline.

The crash-process constructor `AndroidDeviceInfoProvider(context)` passes `{ emptyList() }` as the `entriesProvider` thunk. The closure captures nothing (no reference to any live ModelRegistry), so the crash process has no path to accidentally touch a registry or trigger engine access. The thunk cannot be coerced into returning anything else — it is a compile-time-fixed lambda body. This design decision correctly realizes Decision 10 (crash process has no engine). No risk.
findings.fix: No change. Thunk shape is defensible.

findings.issue: No hardcoded secrets, API keys, tokens, passwords, connection strings, or private keys introduced by this commit. The only string literals touched are (a) ErrorLog component whitelist strings, which are log categorizers, (b) proto field names, (c) error-message templates, (d) the HF URL prefix already present in the codebase.

Build file was touched with +6 lines (per git stat). The diff was not included in the review bundle, but from context (Task 2 spec) this is plumbing for the new migration test source set and/or Hilt plumbing — not a new third-party dependency. No CVE exposure surface added.
findings.fix: No action. If new dependencies were in fact added, re-audit with full gradle diff; current risk assessment assumes no new third-party deps.

notes:
Scope: security audit of commit 6c673d4 on phase/3-history (Task 2 — settings + core fixes). Focus areas confirmed against the task brief:

1. Input-boundary safety (allowlist-poisoned DataStore keys): AllowlistLoader hard-validates `modelId` against `^litert-community/[A-Za-z0-9._-]+$`, rejects `..`, validates commitHash (40 hex), and rejects oversized sizeInBytes. The allowlist is a bundled asset in a signed APK — same trust boundary as the code. Keys written to DataStore (`modelId` post-migration, `name` pre-migration) cannot be tainted at runtime. `name` is unvalidated by regex but stored as a proto3 map key (length-prefixed UTF-8) and sanitized by ErrorLog before ever reaching errors.log. Filed as low severity defense-in-depth only.

2. Atomicity / partial-write risk: FINDING #1 above. Sentinel write is NOT in the same updateData block as the rekey. Crash window exists. Medium severity integrity (A08). Recommend fix but not blocking because: (a) the window is very small — two adjacent DataStore commits on the main DataStore writer, (b) DataStore persists writes aggressively, (c) the blast radius is loss of per-model overrides (prefs), not chats or attachments, (d) recoverable by the user re-adjusting model settings. If treated as a defect it is a high-severity correctness issue; as a security finding it is medium.

3. PII exposure in error logs: orphan-key logging uses `Model.name` as the bare key. Since `name` in the bundled allowlist is non-PII (e.g. `Gemma-4-E4B-it-litert-lm`), and the log is local-only (errors.log in filesDir, user-initiated export only), this is not a PII leak. ErrorLog sanitizes description → no format corruption. Acceptable.

4. DeviceInfoCollector privacy regression: not a regression. modelIds are public, user-initiated export. Confirmed safe.

5. Crash-process constructor shape: `{ emptyList() }` thunk captures no registry reference; crash process has no engine-access path. Decision 10 correctly enforced.

Additional OWASP checks run: A01 (access control — N/A, no auth boundaries), A02 (crypto — N/A), A03 (injection — DataStore keys use proto binary format, no SQL/NoSQL/OS-command injection surface touched), A05 (misconfig — proto3 optional strings default to \"\" which all callers handle; no insecure defaults), A06 (vulnerable components — no new deps), A07 (auth — N/A, local app), A10 (SSRF — no URL construction from user input in this commit; AllowlistLoader's URL building is pre-validated).

Verdict: APPROVED. The atomicity issue (Finding #1) is a real correctness problem worth fixing, but does not rise to a security-blocking level on this local-only, no-attacker-surface code path. Recommend tracking Finding #1 as a follow-up before Wave 2 tasks land that exercise the migration in integration tests. Findings #2 is defense-in-depth for future phases.
